Hi friends =F0=9F=91=8B ,
Happy Monday!=20
Not Boring straddles two worlds - technology and finance. I try to bring pr=
o-level insights to a consumer audience. Today=E2=80=99s essay is about wha=
t happens when those things start to blur, when technology alters finance a=
nd consumers play a larger role in shaping the markets.=20
It=E2=80=99s an early exploration of a topic that I=E2=80=99ll be writing a=
 lot more about in the months and years ahead, putting thoughts down on pap=
er so that we can debate and stress-test them together. I=E2=80=99d love to=
 hear your thoughts in the comments.=20
But first, a word from our sponsor:
Today's Not Boring is brought to you by=E2=80=A6
Seemingly everyone is talking about how next week=E2=80=99s elections will =
impact the markets and their investments. The fear makes sense -- historica=
l data shows US presidential elections tend to make investors more conserva=
tive. Even my most risk-seeking, SPAC-loving friend told me that he=E2=80=
=99s going to cash until after the election. But knowledge is better than f=
ear: do markets perform better in Democratic or Republican administrations?=
 And most importantly, is there an ideal investment strategy during an elec=
tion season?
Weathering financial uncertainty during this election is crucial for long-t=
erm financial success. Yet, it can be challenging to find a go-to source fo=
r trustworthy and unbiased insights. That=E2=80=99s where Zoe Financial com=
es in.
Read Zoe Financial=E2=80=99s Top 5 Investment Impacts of US Presidential El=
ections Here:
Software is Eating the Markets
In 2011, Netscape and a16z founder, Marc Andreesen, wrote a guest post in t=
he WSJ titled Why Software Is Eating the World [https://email.mg2.substack.=
com/c/eJxFkE1uwyAQhU8TltYwgA0LFu2iF-gBLGwmCa0NFuBa7ulLkkWlkebnafTpvdlVuqV82=
i2VyvZCeQze8h61UNIwb6XnWmkWynjNRKsLi615J7bt0xJmV0OKzw8AVOxuJ0GScBAeBhzMdNWo=
NSh_9cPEhZQze3BGt_tAcSZLP5TPFIkt9l7rVi7i7YIfrY7j6I7y1c1pbZvLNcwLlTZ-vjcUcIk=
SlOCcGxBSgwGphl5xRAWGqx6N7IEFi4DAAXuOsley453p_RbTrwqDvkhYb9iVfSrVzd8PFMs2pj=
qlHOKtyd93t9LyFJrdsfV1j6GeI0U3LeRfSdRXdk9j9dzIRjrKQrVSfh0f8UihBsUNazSfWorxH=
_QHCXV9ww]. With nine years of hindsight, he was so obviously right that it=
=E2=80=99s clich=C3=A9 to cite it today. I=E2=80=99m kind of mad at myself =
for typing these words right now.=C2=A0
Software has fundamentally altered nearly every industry. Netflix put a den=
t in cable=E2=80=99s long standing dominance, and already TikTok and Fortni=
te threaten Netflix. Newspapers are dying. Millions of people now earn livi=
ngs in the Gig Economy or the Passion Economy. A private company, SpaceX, i=
s doing things that NASA can=E2=80=99t. The list goes on.=C2=A0
But despite that, we operate under the assumption that financial markets ar=
e somehow different, impervious. Of course, technology has impacted finance=
=2E High-speed trading accounts for an i=
ncreasingly large percentage of activ=
ity, and institutional investors will spend millions or billions on any tec=
h that gets them a slight advantage. But if recent tech history is a guide,=
 technology=E2=80=99s ultimate impact on finance will be less about further=
 entrenching incumbents; it will empower consumers.=C2=A0
Consumers are participating directly in the market in greater numbers than =
ever before, but every analysis I=E2=80=99ve read treats increased retail p=
articipation as a temporary COVID-induced blip, and retail traders as irres=
ponsible and irrational gamblers just looking to have a good time. Soon, se=

for them. The time between hearing about a hot new stock and being able to =
execute a trade fell to practically zero. But the online brokerages=E2=80=
=99 interfaces were simplistic, and the ways for =E2=80=9Cinvestors=E2=80=
=9D to find and communicate with each other limited to clunky, text-based m=
essage boards. They weren=E2=80=99t buying new experiences, just trying not=
 to miss out on the gains their neighbors were generating. They moved money=
 from savings, brokerages, or 401ks into accounts they managed, and investe=
d in anything with a =E2=80=9C.com=E2=80=9D in its name.=C2=A0
In other words, they changed how they invested the same pool of investment =
funds, personally directing investments towards riskier trades, but didn=E2=
=80=99t re-allocate money from buying things or experiences. In fact, perso=
nal consumption expenditures grew at slightly higher rates in 1999 than in =
the years leading up to the bubble.
Instead, investors moved money from safer assets, like bonds, into riskier =
ones, like shares of Pets.com, despite the fact that bonds=E2=80=99 expecte=
d returns were higher than stocks=E2=80=99. We all know how that ended.=C2=
=A0
Today is different. People are looking for new ways to spend money they wou=
ld have otherwise spent on new clothes, trips, dining, and all of the thing=
s that people valued before the pandemic. It even makes sense for most peop=
le to move allocations away from bonds -- the most boring asset class -- be=
cause of low rates. Concurrently, new platforms are changing both what asse=
ts retail investors can buy and their experience owning those assets.=C2=A0
That has major implications on the prices that people will pay and the retu=
rns they=E2=80=99ll accept. Because the expected long-term return on many g=
oods and experiences is zero -- you don=E2=80=99t expect to get $120 back a=
fter going to a concert for which you paid $100 -- treating investments par=
tially as a consumption good and partially as an experience should mean tha=
t consumer investors are willing to pay higher prices, or conversely, to ge=
t lower returns.
This won=E2=80=99t just drive up the value of hot stocks. Prices are not ne=
arly as crazy as they were during the dot com bubble, and they won=E2=80=99=
t be. It will represent a shift from consumption into investments on new pl=
atforms and in new asset classes.=C2=A0
To read more about the new platforms and asset classes reshaping the way co=
nsumers invest, and to understand whether =E2=80=9Cit=E2=80=99s different t=
his time=E2=80=9D:

line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">In 2011, Netscape and a16z founder, =
Marc Andreesen, wrote a guest post in the <em>WSJ</em> titled <em><a href=
=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFkE1uwyAQhU8TltYwgA0LFu2iF-gBLGwmCa=
0NFuBa7ulLkkWlkebnafTpvdlVuqV82i2VyvZCeQze8h61UNIwb6XnWmkWynjNRKsLi615J7bt0=

zIRjrKQrVSfh0f8UihBsUNazSfWorxH_QHCXV9ww" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-dec=
oration: underline;">Why Software Is Eating the World</a></em>. With nine y=
ears of hindsight, he was so obviously right that it’s cliché to=
 cite it today. I’m kind of mad at myself for typing these words righ=
t now. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 2=
6px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Software has fundamentally altered nearly every in=
dustry. Netflix put a dent in cable’s long standing dominance, and al=
ready TikTok and Fortnite threaten Netflix. Newspapers are dying. Millions =
of people now earn livings in the Gig Economy or the Passion Economy. A pri=
vate company, SpaceX, is doing things that NASA can’t. The list goes =
on. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px=
; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">But despite that, we operate under the assumption tha=
t financial markets are somehow different, impervious. Of course, technolog=
y has impacted finance. High-speed trading accounts for an increasingly lar=
ge percentage of activity, and institutional investors will spend millions =
or billions on any tech that gets them a slight advantage. But if recent te=
ch history is a guide, technology’s ultimate impact on finance will b=
e less about further entrenching incumbents; it will empower consumers.&nbs=
p;</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margi=
n: 0 0 1em 0;">Consumers are participating directly in the market in greate=
r numbers than ever before, but every analysis I’ve read treats incre=
ased retail participation as a temporary COVID-induced blip, and retail tra=
ders as irresponsible and irrational gamblers just looking to have a good t=
ime. Soon, serious people argue, things will go back to the way they were.<=
/p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: =
0 0 1em 0;">They may be right. But what if they’re not? </p><h1 =
style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-family: 'SF Compact Display', -apple-system, =
BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', Roboto, Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif, 'Appl=

eight: 700; line-height: 1.16em; margin: 1em 0 0.625em 0;"><strong>Investin=
g in Social Status</strong></h1><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px=
; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">In November, back when San Francis=
co was still more physical place than state of mind and most people associa=
ted Corona with lime, Alex Danco wrote an excellent post called <em><a href=
=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUMuOhCAQ_JrhpgFERg4c5rK_YRBaJYNgoJ=
1d9-sXZw6bdPpdXemyBmFJ-dR7KkiOAnn0TjPJh64XijgtHBv6gfgyzhlgMz5ozAeQ_ZiCtwZ9i=
m8EpbwnqzaMTbNScqYT77ikQBVXlslBdWYAOZOLZzSH8xAtaHhBPlMEEvSKuJdb97jxr2omwI8z=
0abWpq3WnDJVA2NXfq8OV2hKst6EphxT8e5s0tyYuEBofHxBQR-Xuke85rSiKZeMC9mLlrVKuj2=
m397fh5ug28Lb6wQa-7zYSNYx4ZTydUDQ52o2CO9B_XmscTuix3OEaKYA7iMHfgR8f4fnDjrCdw=
mACPnTvDQSXX_vmSKVzaUqZfwn-gOXvoP5" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoratio=
n: underline;">The Social Subsidy of Angel Investing</a></em>. </p><p =
style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1e=
m 0;">In it, he argued three main points: </p><ol style=3D"margin: 1em=
 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet=
;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: =
0;">Angel Investors in the Bay Area don’t just invest for the financi=
al returns; they also invest for the social returns. </p></li><li styl=
e=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">The soc=
ial rewards of angel investing solve an important chicken-and-egg problem i=
n early stage fundraising that financial rewards do not.</p></li><li style=
=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D=
"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">The social=
 returns to angel investing have a strong geographical network effect, beca=
use they require a threshold density in order to kick in.</p></li></ol><p s=
tyle=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em=
 0;">In San Francisco, owning a Ferrari wasn’t nearly as cool as owni=
ng pre-seed shares in Stripe or Uber. In some ways, startup equity behaved =
more like a Veblen Good -- one that paradoxically sees more demand as the p=
rice goes up -- than like an investment. How else to explain the mad dash t=
o invest in Clubhouse at any price? A high price signaled a more competitiv=
e round, which meant more social status for getting in.</p><p style=3D"colo=
r: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">To an o=
utsider, this might seem crazy. Why invest your hard-earned money in someth=
ing that doesn’t provide the best risk-adjusted returns? When angel i=
nvestors wrote checks into startups, they were really buying two things: an=
 asset with a potential financial return and a status symbol. </p><tab=

line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">It’s easy to make fun of the n=
ew wave of Robinhood-using, SPAC-buying, WallStreetBets-posting “inve=
stors.” They’re not doing the type of deep analysis traders wou=
ld do, choosing to invest with the herd instead of uncovering their own hid=
den value gems.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height=
: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">One popular view of this phenomenon is that in =
a market like this, Robinhood is just a casino. Gamblers know the odds are =
against them, but they do it anyway, for the rush or the small chance of a =
huge return. That’s certainly part of it. </p><p style=3D"color:=
 #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">But this =
retail trader trend has persisted for nearly a year, around the world, and =
I think there’s something more to it. Retail investors value differen=
t things than institutional ones. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font=
-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">In <em><a href=3D"https=
://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUEGOwyAMfE24NQICJBw47GW_ERFwE1QCEThbZV-_tD2=
sZGkkj-3xjLMIay6XOXJFclYoc_CGKT4NUmjijfBskhMJdb4XgN2GaLCcQI5zicFZDDm9Nyjlkm=

ziy79C_0BRCqAdg" style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; text-decoration: underline;">What=
 Clayton Christensen Got Wrong</a></em>, one of the earliest Stratechery pi=
eces, Ben Thompson critiqued the titular HBS professor and creator of disru=
ption theory’s prediction that the iPod and iPhone were doomed to fai=
l. Christensen argued that while Apple’s integrated approach makes se=
nse in the early days of a new market, over time, as the products’ co=
mponents become commoditized, a more modular approach wins out. When even m=
odular products become “good enough,” the low-cost solution dom=
inates the market.</p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-hei=
ght: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">What he got wrong, according to Thompson, wa=
s assuming that consumers and businesses make decisions in the same way. Sp=
ecifically, Thompson highlighted three of Christensen’s flawed assump=
tions:</p><ol style=3D"margin: 1em 0;"><li style=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px=
 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size:=
 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Buyers are rational.</p></li><li styl=
e=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Every a=
ttribute that matters can be documented and measured. </p></li><li sty=
le=3D"margin: 3.75px 0 3.75px 7.5px; mso-special-format: bullet;"><p style=
=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0;">Modular=
 providers can become “good enough” on all the attributes that =
matter to buyers.</p></li></ol><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px;=
 line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Those assumptions largely hold for =
business buyers, but not for consumers. Business buyers care about getting =
all the features they need at the lowest possible cost. Consumers care abou=
t user experience, and how a product makes them feel. </p><p style=3D"=
color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Ins=
titutional investors still account for the vast majority of dollars traded.=
 Even though retail traders’ share of daily average revenue trades (D=
ARTs), according to <a href=3D"https://email.mg2.substack.com/c/eJxFUcGO6yA=

y brought more people into the market and encouraged new behavior. Unwatche=
d by the professionals, retail investors could make risky gambles that they=
 would be too embarrassed to tell their broker to put in for them. The time=
 between hearing about a hot new stock and being able to execute a trade fe=
ll to practically zero. But the online brokerages’ interfaces were si=
mplistic, and the ways for “investors” to find and communicate =
with each other limited to clunky, text-based message boards. They weren&#8=
217;t buying new experiences, just trying not to miss out on the gains thei=
r neighbors were generating. They moved money from savings, brokerages, or =
401ks into accounts they managed, and invested in anything with a “.c=
om” in its name. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px=
; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">In other words, they changed how t=
hey invested the same pool of investment funds, personally directing invest=
ments towards riskier trades, but didn’t re-allocate money from buyin=
g things or experiences. In fact, personal consumption expenditures grew at=
 slightly higher rates in 1999 than in the years leading up to the bubble.<=
/p><table class=3D"image-wrapper" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=

t: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Instead, investors moved money from safer asse=
ts, like bonds, into riskier ones, like shares of Pets.com, <em>despite the=
 fact that bonds’ expected returns were higher than stocks’. </=
em>We all know how that ended. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-si=
ze: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">Today is different. People=
 are looking for new ways to spend money they would have otherwise spent on=
 new clothes, trips, dining, and all of the things that people valued befor=
e the pandemic. It even makes sense for most people to move allocations awa=
y from bonds -- the most boring asset class -- because of low rates. Concur=
rently, new platforms are changing both what assets retail investors can bu=
y and their experience owning those assets. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a=
1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;">That has majo=
r implications on the prices that people will pay and the returns they&#821=
7;ll accept. Because the expected long-term return on many goods and experi=
ences is zero -- you don’t expect to get $120 back after going to a c=
oncert for which you paid $100 -- treating investments partially as a consu=
mption good and partially as an experience should mean that consumer invest=
ors are willing to pay higher prices, or conversely, to get lower returns.<=
/p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: =
0 0 1em 0;">This won’t just drive up the value of hot stocks. Prices =
are not nearly as crazy as they were during the dot com bubble, and they wo=
n’t be. It will represent a shift from consumption into investments o=
n new platforms and in new asset classes. </p><p style=3D"color: #1a1a=
1a; font-size: 16px; line-height: 26px; margin: 0 0 1em 0;"><em>To read mor=
